data.title: Wolfspeed Hold Rating: Debt, Cash Drain, And Bankruptcy Keep The Future In Doubt
data.description:
Wolfspeed, Inc. (NSYE:WOLF) is living through a make-or-break moment. At today’s price, the market clearly expects the worst and I completely get why. The company’s debt is massive, the cash burn is intense and there is a bankruptcy process already in motion. At the same time, Wolfspeed is not just another chip company with a generic product. The tech here actually matters for things like electric vehicles and renewables, which gives it at least a fighting chance to survive this mess, if everything lines up just right.

Right now, I don’t see a strong reason to buy or to sell. The risk of losing it all is very real for anyone holding the stock but on the flip side, the upside is only possible if the restructuring actually works and Wolfspeed manages to fix its business without completely crushing shareholders. For most investors, I believe the right call is to stay put and watch how this drama unfolds. Jumping in fresh would be rolling the dice and selling out here feels a bit like throwing in the towel at the bottom, unless you simply can’t stomach the risk.

What Wolfspeed Does and Where It Stands

Wolfspeed calls itself a leader in silicon carbide and gallium nitride chips and to be fair, its products have a real role in the next wave of electric vehicles, fast-charging stations and some power applications that regular silicon just can’t handle. The company’s roots run deep and it has tried to create a special place in the chip world. But none of that shields it from the situation it faces now.

The truth is, Wolfspeed is in survival mode. After taking on more than $6 billion in debt and burning through its cash, it had to throw in the towel and file for bankruptcy protection earlier this summer. There have been a lot of leadership changes, including new people in the CFO and COO seats who seem to have one job: keep the company alive and get the finances under control. Wolfspeed did sell its RF business to make things more manageable but it is still a complicated business to run. Right now, it is less about beating the competition with new ideas and more about surviving.

The Market Wolfspeed Is In

If you step back, the industry Wolfspeed operates in is actually exciting. Silicon carbide and gallium nitride are the future for a lot of power electronics. Big names like Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY) and ON Semiconductor (ON) have noticed this too and are building out their own production lines. That means Wolfspeed is not the only one racing to win contracts for the next round of electric vehicles and clean energy projects. There are also big swings in revenue depending on when customers decide to upgrade their tech or start new projects, which makes things even bumpier.

While government support like the CHIPS Act has given the whole sector a boost, not every company is built to last. Wolfspeed’s technology is valuable but these days, the ability to raise cash and keep the lights on is just as important as having a cool product.

How the Numbers Look

The financial story here is about as rough as it gets. Wolfspeed brought in $185 million in revenue for the most recent quarter, which is down compared to last year. Margins are deep in the red, with gross losses and operating losses both piling up. The company lost almost $285 million in just three months and close to a billion dollars over the past nine months.

Cash is disappearing quickly, dropping from $2.2 billion last summer to about $1.3 billion in March, even as debt sits above $6.5 billion. The latest numbers also show that shareholders’ equity has been slashed to just above $200 million. The company itself admits that there is “serious doubt” about its ability to keep going unless the restructuring works. If you are looking for any sort of financial cushion, you won’t find it here.

Is the Stock Cheap or Not?

It’s tough to use traditional valuation math with Wolfspeed. Sure, the price is slightly above book value but the company is burning so much cash that book value could shrink even more or get wiped out entirely. Forward earnings estimates are deeply negative for the next couple of years, so things like price-to-earnings are meaningless right now. Even the company’s market value, which is down to $234 million, looks tiny next to the mountain of debt it owes. The enterprise value is over seven times sales, which would be rich for a healthy business, never mind one in the middle of bankruptcy. Even after falling over 90 percent, I just don’t see a clear reason to call Wolfspeed a bargain.

What the News and Market Say

If you’ve followed the headlines, you know how wild it’s been. The restructuring news at the end of June caused a brief spike in the stock price but it didn’t last. Stories about bankruptcy, lawsuits and funds selling their shares have taken over the news feed. Leadership changes are coming fast but it feels like crisis management, not a turnaround. From where I sit, the market expects disaster and has already priced in a lot of pain. The only folks still in the game are speculators looking to make a quick buck on big swings.

The Big Risks to Watch

Let’s be honest: the risks are huge. Bankruptcy could wipe out existing shareholders or dilute them to the point that even a rebound doesn’t help much. The cash burn is out of control and with creditors now calling the shots, shareholders have very little power. Bigger companies are making moves in silicon carbide and Wolfspeed is playing catch-up with less cash and more distractions. Legal fights, class actions and the need to raise new money all make things worse. Any talk of a buyout or “too important to fail” scenario is more wishful thinking than an actual plan at this stage.

What Could Happen Next

For the next six months, everything depends on how the restructuring turns out. The company says it can keep running for the next year but that assumes it can pull off asset sales and get creditors to play ball. If Wolfspeed makes it through this period, there is still a long-term market for its products. The problem is that analyst estimates show the company losing money for years to come, so there’s no quick fix in sight. Operational improvements will take time and even then, the company has to convince customers and investors that it’s here to stay.

Different Paths from Here

When I look at possible futures, the worst-case scenario is clear: the restructuring fails, creditors take over and shareholders end up with nothing or next to nothing. The most likely outcome, in my opinion, is a severely diluted turnaround where the company limps along and maybe, just maybe, claws its way back over time. There’s also a chance, slim as it is, that Wolfspeed pulls off a miracle, manages the restructuring, keeps customers and wins new business, all while raising enough money to survive. In that best case, the stock could bounce but any rally would likely be from a much lower base after all the dust settles.

My Bottom Line

To wrap it up, I see Wolfspeed as a textbook example of a “wait and see” story. The market has already given up hope but that doesn’t make the stock a steal. With the bankruptcy process and debt overhang, there’s a real risk of losing your entire investment. At the same time, it feels too late to panic and sell unless you just want to move on. If the company manages to shed its debt and rebuild, maybe there’s a shot at a turnaround but I wouldn’t rush in now. Personally, I’d keep watching from the sidelines.

data.title: Q2 Earnings: BTC Gains Trump Rising Costs For Hut 8
data.description:
As we approach five months since my last Seeking Alpha article covering Hut 8 Corp. (NASDAQ:HUT), we have two additional quarters of company performance to assess in addition to the performance of the stock price. Readers might recall that back in March I felt investors would do better being long Bitcoin (BTC-USD) rather than HUT shares:

While I'm encouraged by Hut 8's ability to transform the business over the last year or two, the equity still goes as Bitcoin goes. And if given a choice between the two, investors will likely do much better just buying Bitcoin.

As fate would have it, that call has turned out to be wrong:

Since my last HUT piece, the stock - as well as the broader mining sector - outperformed Bitcoin by a rather significant margin. I think it's worth looking at HUT's recent earnings report to estimate whether this out-performance can reasonably continue.

Q2-25 Earnings

For quarter-ended June 2025, Hut 8 reported $41.3 million in top line revenue. This was a 17.3% year over year increase from June 2024. By segment, the revenue breakout was as follows:

Revenue 000s 3mo 6/30/25 3mo 6/30/24 YOY
Power $5,492 $10,530 -47.8%
Digital Infrastructure $1,512 $5,264 -71.3%
Compute $34,295 $15,795 117.1%
Other $0 $3,626 -100.0%
Total Revenue $41,299 $35,215 17.3%

Source: Hut 8.

These numbers are a bit all over the place due to Hut 8's reclassification and consolidation of certain revenue segments. Underneath the "Power" umbrella, Hut 8 generates revenue from capacity contracts and energy sales to the Ontario electrical grid. Under this same segment, Hut 8 offers its energy infrastructure expertise to institutional clients. Within Hut 8's "Digital Infrastructure" segment, the company offers co-location services for both Bitcoin mining and CPU-based workloads. "Compute" rolls up three different sub-segments that include Bitcoin mining - which has been spun out to American Bitcoin - data center cloud services, and Highrise AI; the latter of which runs a GPU-as-a-service model.

As would likely be expected given the operations of the legacy Hut 8 entity and the biggest contributor to top line revenue in the table above, Compute was Hut's largest expense with $14.6 million in COGS in quarter-ended June.

COGS 000s 3mo 6/30/25 3mo 6/30/24 YOY
Power $5,000 $5,449 -8.2%
Digital Infrastructure $2,120 $4,331 -51.1%
Compute $14,656 $8,670 69.0%
Other $0 $2,186 -100.0%
Total COGS $21,776 $20,636 5.5%

Source: Hut 8.

Compute COGS grew year over year by 69%, which was actually quite a bit less than the 117% year over year growth in revenue from that category. This led to a monster gain in gross profit for Compute from $7.1 million last year to $19.6 million this year. That said, the year over year performance in the other segments looks quite rough:

Gross Profit 000s 3mo 6/30/25 3mo 6/30/24 YOY
Power $492 $5,081 -90.3%
Digital Infrastructure -$608 $933 -165.2%
Compute $19,639 $7,125 175.6%
Other $0 $1,440 -100.0%
Gross Profit $19,523 $14,579 33.9%

Source: Hut 8.

Again, this is due to segment consolidation and reclassification to some degree. But gross profit in Power fell by 90% year over year and Digital Infrastructure turned negative. This was largely impacted by the termination of an agreement with Ionic Digital. Compute remains the biggest driver of the top line for Hut 8 and that segment is still highly reliant on Bitcoin mining. As has been a common tale in mining earnings reports for the last few quarters, positive net income is generally only possible with paper gains in Bitcoin holdings.

During the quarter, Hut 8 reported a positive net income of $137.5 million. Take away the $217.6 million gain on BTC holdings and Hut 8's real operations were actually negative to the tune of $80.2 million in the quarter. I recognize that FASB accounting rules allow for this, but I do think it can be misleading to include unrealized gains on dormant assets in a company's operational performance as it can potentially give investors a false view of the company's true profitability. And if BTC asset revaluation is going to be the key determinant in Hut's corporate performance, then the level of BTC relative to the market valuation of the equity may be important to consider.

mNAV, Sats Per Share, and Ownership

While HUT is not a 'treasury company' in the same sense that an entity like Strategy (MSTR) would be considered one, Hut 8 does command the 12th largest BTC-stack held by any public company with 10,667 BTC as of quarter-ended June.

Given the company's current market value, HUT has an mNAV of 1.76. This is close to the mNAV of Strategy and generally in-line with other mining stocks like Riot Platforms (RIOT) and CleanSpark (CLSK). Even as the company has often relied on shareholder dilution to fund growth, the year over year change in the "sats per share" figure shown below has grown 2% year over year from 10,017 to 10,217.

This is well below the YoY sat-backing growth that we've seen from companies like MARA Holdings (MARA) or Riot Platforms but ahead of similarly sized Cipher Mining (CIFR). It should also be noted that recent "sats per share" numbers over the last few quarters have been roughly flat and well below the highs from 2022.

Something else that I like to look at with the public miners is the degree to which hedge funds are holding the stocks. As of Q1 25, 30% of HUT's outstanding shares are held by the "smart money."

This 30% hedge fund ownership generally puts HUT in the middle of the pack relative to the 12 stocks shown above, but much closer to top-owned Core Scientific (CORZ) than HIVE Digital Technologies (HIVE). My read on this is that even though Hut 8 is seen as one of the stronger bets in the public mining space by fund managers.

Risk To Consider

We've already seen Hut is not a profitable company without BTC-revaluation. Despite that, Compute has actually seen growth in gross margins year over year. From where I sit, a big factor holding back Hut from positive net income in more real terms are the ballooning expenses in SG&A and Depreciation & Amortization:

Both categories have seen growth outpace total revenue over the last year. But it's the level to which the spending is occurring relative to top line revenue that I find more concerning. In Q2 24, SG&A was 44.5% of revenue - which is still quite high. That figure ballooned to 89.7% in Q1-25 and 73% last quarter. While lower on a relative basis, Depreciation and Amortization came in at 68.3% and 47.1% of revenue over the last two quarters. To be clear, Hut is not the worst offender of SG&A as a percentage of revenue, but the trend is worrisome if the HPC/AI data center build out turns out to be a bust.

Closing Takeaways

I think the major concern here is fairly straightforward; it takes unrealized gains in Bitcoins that have already been mined to generate positive net income in the reporting quarter. This has the double impact of masking operational losses while also putting the company at significant risk if the value of that Bitcoin were to actually go down substantially - as has been the case numerous times over the coins's nearly 16 year history.

There are likely some who view Bitcoin cycle theory as less relevant in a post-ETF world, but Bitcoin the asset is currently reliant on capital flows rather than underlying usage of the network itself. As easily as this capital has moved into BTC, it can most assuredly move out as well. That said, Bitcoin's current trajectory still appears to be up. If the company can continue to grow "sats per share" the way it has over the last quarter, then it stands to reason that HUT could theoretically continue to outperform BTC in a bull run. As a raw BTC-proxy, I like other opportunities better. And as an HPC/AI investment, there are other companies that are closer to pure play. I'm still going to rate HUT shares a Hold today.

data.title: EWA: A Guide To This Australian Large-Cap ETF
data.description:
Introduction To The iShares MSCI Australia ETF

The iShares MSCI Australia ETF (NYSEARCA:EWA), which is backed by Blackrock, Inc., is a $1.5 billion-sized product that covers roughly 50 stocks from the Australian equity market. EWA, which will complete three decades as a listed product in March 2026, offers a solid enough yield of over 3% and can be picked up at an expense ratio of 0.50%.

What Does EWA Do?

Principally, EWA tracks the MSCI Australia Index (MAI), which is designed to serve as a barometer of Australian equities for foreign investors. The index aims to cover 85% of the total free-float market capitalization of the Australian markets.

Ewa tracks this index through representative sampling. This means EWA won’t necessarily own every single stock that MAI covers, and also not in the same proportion. Rather, it will only choose to cover a certain sample of stocks that, in aggregate, end up having similar features as the index. These features include the average market cap, the risk profile, the fundamental characteristics, the economic characteristics, etc. Also, EWA typically only sets out to ensure that 80% of its assets include the component equities of MAI; the remaining 20% could even include non-MAI equities, but also some derivative products and money-market funds.

Usually passive ETFs that follow the representative sampling strategy (rather than fully replicating the holdings of their underlying index) will typically tend to post rather wide tracking errors. The tracking error basically gives you a sense of how different an ETF's returns are relative to the returns of its underlying index. In EWA’s case, we can see that the tracking error has widened over the past year (and is well above the ETF median error rates of 7.4%), compared to what was seen over a three-year duration (roughly in line with the ETF median).

What Are The Main Characteristics Of EWA’s Portfolio?

Even though EWA’s tracking index is believed to cover 85% of the Australian equities market cap (on a free-float basis), since it assigns portfolio weights on the respective market cap of the stocks (larger market caps garner higher weights), it is heavily tilted towards giant and large-cap Australian stocks, which account for a whopping ~96% of the total portfolio. While mid-caps account for a marginal proportion, Aussie small-caps don’t get any representation.

Compounding the giant-cap tilt, we also have a scenario where 10 stocks alone, or roughly one-fifth of the portfolio (in terms of numbers), take up a hefty weight of 63% in aggregate, or close to two-thirds of the portfolio in value terms.

There is a very clear sector concentration effect in play with financial stocks dominating this portfolio: 5 out of the top 10 stocks from this portfolio are financials, while over 40% of the portfolio comes from this sector alone.

Who Is EWA Meant For?

EWA is meant for investors who are looking for concentrated exposure to Australian giant caps and are prepared to overlook the seemingly alluring growth runway that one would typically associate with mid-caps and small caps. These mid-caps may be intrinsically riskier in their movements (relative to giant-caps), but a lot of them are still closer to their infancy and likely far from hitting a saturation point in their overall development lifecycle. An overexposure to Australian giant-caps will also imply a predilection towards more diversified business models that aren’t strictly reliant on prospects in the domestic economy alone and are also closely impacted by developments across the world.

Investors who are looking to own a stable basket of stocks for the long haul will also likely gravitate to EWA because it is not a portfolio that undertakes a great deal of churn on an annual basis. Most ETFs turnover around 30% of their portfolio every year, but in EWA’s just a minuscule component of 5% gets turned over.

EWA would also appeal to someone who is having a hard time choosing between large-cap growth and value-style stocks, as both these buckets have an equal weight, with blended large-caps (those that have both growth and value characteristics taking up the largest share) dominating.

For the uninitiated, growth style stocks are characterized by high monthly price momentum, strong revenue growth rates, steep valuations, high retention ratios, limited shareholder distributions, etc., and value style stocks are characterized by cheap valuations (be it on a price-to-earnings basis, price-to-book value basis, price-to-cash flow basis, etc.), high dividend yields and payouts, low revenue growth profiles, etc.

Investors who are also comfortable with forgoing frequent dividend distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis will like EWA, as it distributes only on a semi-annual basis.

Given the strong tilt towards financial stocks, it’s also fair to say that EWA could be seen as an imperfect proxy for those looking to bet on the interest rate policies of the Australian central bank.

What Are The Risks Associated With EWA?

Foreign exchange-related risks are often an underappreciated facet of international investing. Even though EWA is priced in USD, the stocks it holds are denominated in AUD. The Aussie dollar is often perceived to be a risk-on currency—a currency that flourishes when risk appetite in the financial markets is high and suffers when risk appetite is on the wane. And, when risk-on conditions are not present in the global markets, it tends to suffer. A decline in the AUD will be detrimental for holders of EWA, as the ETF does not hedge its portfolio. Thus, when the Aussie-denominated assets are converted to USD, holders would end up receiving less than what they would normally have received in local currency (AUD) terms.

Ostensibly, one of the reasons why US investors would shift to Australian stocks is because they’re assuming they are getting a bit of diversification away from domestic stocks. EWA may not be a suitable developed market alternative, as its beta over the last 3 years (relative to the S&P500) of over 1x suggests that it pretty much moves in tandem with the US markets. Note that all the other major developed market ETFs have beta readings (vs. the S&P500) that are much lower than EWA. For those not familiar, the beta tells you how sensitive a product’s movements are to the movement of a tracking index.

Another key risk is that EWA’s valuations appear to be quite pricey, particularly in light of the long-term earnings prospects of its holdings, and this may dampen further investor sentiment. As per Morningstar data, EWA is priced at an elevated price-to-earnings multiple of close to 20x, which represents a 5% premium over global markets and an even steeper premium of 35% over developed markets outside the US. Normally, when equities are priced at steep valuation multiples, it’s because they are poised to deliver a lucrative trajectory of earnings growth. However, EWA’s holdings are currently poised to deliver less than 7% earnings growth, which is 200-300 bps lower than the other two options.

Is There An Alternative Australian-Focused ETF To EWA?

Besides EWA, Aussie-focused investors are also afforded the opportunity of pursuing another passive ETF called the Franklin FTSE Australia ETF (FLAU).

FLAU is substantially smaller in scope (only $70 million of total AUM, which is a pittance compared to EWA’s $1.5 billion) and has a much shorter listing history of less than eight years, as opposed to nearly three decades for EWA. It tracks a different index to EWA called the FTSE Australia Capped Index.

The main difference to note is that FLAU’s portfolio is wider in scope, covering over 100 Australian stocks, but once again the onus is on giant caps, although a double-digit exposure to mid-caps is welcome (unlike EWA). FLAU also stands out for its remarkably cheap expense ratio of just 0.09%, which is not even one-fifth the cost of EWA.

Both products offer yields in excess of 3%, but the smaller ETF has the slight edge. Also note that both products don’t resort to a great deal of churn (as exemplified by an annual turnover rate of mid-single digits).

However, FLAU’s lack of popularity is very evident in the daily dollar volumes that it sees. It trades only around $200 thousand per day and is a long shot from what EWA trades daily. This ends up leaving an inimical mark on the average spreads that FLAU witnesses between its bid and the ask. An average spread of 0.74% is very concerning, whereas EWA’s is perfectly acceptable at just 0.03%.

Summary

EWA, which almost has a three-decade history of covering Australian giants and large caps, is currently the largest and most liquid Australian-focused ETF in the market. However, EWA is a pricey product (both in structure and the valuation of its holdings), has strong concentration effects, and may not serve as the most appropriate diversifier for those looking for developed market alternatives from the US markets.

This article answers these three main questions about EWA:

  • Which investor will find EWA appealing?
  • What are the risks associated with EWA?
  • How Does EWA Compare to FLAU, the other Australian-focused ETF?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the Fund for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about this ETF.

data.title: WTI Crude Forecast: Risk Premium Fades, Supply Pressures Mount, Bearish Trend Ahead
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The geopolitical risk premium in the oil market has faded, taking a back seat after a four-week, 30% parabolic rally in West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy for WTI crude futures) during the initial phase of the Israel-Iran conflict.

US crude oil inventories are building up again

The growth of US crude oil inventories excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) on a year-on-year basis has an indirect correlation with the movement of WTI crude oil, as a build-up in oil inventories puts downside pressure on oil prices.

Since 20 June 2025, the drawn down of US crude oil inventories (excluding SPR) has slowed down from -9.9% y/y to -1.3% y/y as of 1 August 2025 based on data from the US Energy Information Administration (EIA) which suggests a potential build-up in oil inventories which is likely to put further downside pressure on the prices of WTI crude oil (see Fig. 1).

A possible reduction of US sanctions on Russian oil

Recent media reports have highlighted that the Russian government confirmed that Presidents Putin and Trump will meet for summit talks on ending the war in Ukraine in the next few days.

Hence, a ceasefire deal between Russia and Ukraine is likely to allow the removal or reduction of sanctions on Russia’s oil exports, in turn, increasing oil supply on top of ongoing OPEC+ production hikes where the cartel has agreed to pump an extra 2.5 million barrels of oil per day starting in September.

The net effect is a more dampening effect on the prices of WTI crude oil.

The three-month corrective rebound in WTI crude oil may have ended

The West Texas Oil CFD has broken below its 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages. In addition, its daily MACD trend indicator has broken below a former parallel ascending support from 6 May 2025 and continued to trend downwards below its centreline.

These observations suggest that a three-month corrective rebound from the 9 April 2025 low to the 23 June 2025 high is likely to have ended. The next possible movement of the West Texas Oil CFD is likely a medium-term (multi-week) impulsive bearish down move within a major downtrend phase in place since the 28 September 2023 high (see Fig. 2).

Bearish bias below US$67.25/68.80 key medium-term pivotal resistance for the next supports to come in at US$60.55, US$55.00, and US$50.50/49.10 (congestion area of 5 June/7 Aug 2017 & Fibonacci extension).

However, a clearance above US$68.80 invalidates the bearish scenario for a squeeze up to retest the next medium-term resistances at US$71.30 and US$74.00.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AI, Energy And Everything In Between: Thematics In July 2025
data.description:
Building on the launch of the inaugural S&P Thematics Dashboard, the July edition analyzes the monthly performance of a broad set of themes to uncover key market dynamics. Designed as a thematic market lens, the dashboard—developed in collaboration with Theia Insights—offers rich perspectives on market performance by leveraging nearly 200 themes shaping the global economy. It highlights top- and bottom-performing themes, integrates macro and sentiment indicators and delivers multi-lens insights across regions, sectors, megatrends and factors.

This month’s thematic performance was shaped by AI-driven infrastructure demand, renewed investor enthusiasm for precision health technologies and sustained momentum in the energy transition space. In contrast, traditional healthcare segments (e.g., Healthcare Insurance) and consumer-linked themes underperformed. Additionally, recent legislation related to the crypto sector fueled investor optimism across much of the crypto ecosystem—particularly within the Stablecoins theme.

Infrastructure of Intelligence: AI Push Fuels Computing Demand

At the top of the leaderboard, Data Centers & High-Performance Computing posted a standout 13.40% monthly return, adding to its YTD surge (up 32.06%). Meta (META) and Google’s (GOOGL) multi-billion-dollar commitments to AI infrastructure, referred to as “AI factories,” were received positively by the markets. Their revenues showed gains partly due to this significant capital expenditure. Limited access to reliable power and grid capacity for energy-intensive AI infrastructure, once a critical bottleneck, is now being actively addressed through a wave of investment in nuclear energy and renewable-plus-storage solutions. These themes also delivered strong returns in July, as outlined later in the document.

Adjacent AI infrastructure sectors also benefited, with Optical Communications & Optoelectronics rising 10.65% to deliver strong double-digit returns, driven by record-setting breakthroughs in data transmission speeds and silicon photonics integration.

Healthcare Divide: Innovation Outperforms while Services Falter

A sharp divergence defined the healthcare space.

On one end, innovation-led biotech themes gained meaningful traction.

  • Cannabis & Psychedelics rose 13.23%, helped by clinical data validating psilocybin’s therapeutic potential and regulatory progress in Europe.
  • Genomics & Gene Editing gained 11.59%, as breakthroughs in AI-enabled gene diagnostics and newborn genome screening expanded the sector’s relevance.
  • Cancer Therapy also performed strongly, rising 10.36%, buoyed by developments in precision medicine.

In contrast, Health Insurance recorded the steepest decline, at –10.71%. Growing concern over rising costs for insurers and regulatory scrutiny, weighed heavily on traditional healthcare companies. The Pharmacy theme also decreased 9.92%, as falling reimbursement rates, inflation, softer consumer spending and rising competition continue to squeeze margins.

Energy Storage and “Old” Fuel Find New Purpose

Batteries & Energy Storage gained 11.61%, continuing a four-month streak of positive returns. The surge was fueled by accelerating grid-scale storage deployments, particularly those utilizing lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries, which supported lithium demand while reducing reliance on more expensive nickel and cobalt chemistries. This dynamic favored companies operating across the value chain, from lithium producers to residential and utility-scale integrators.

Coal’s 9.12% rise marked an interesting shift, underpinned by supportive policy dynamics and news of potential curbs on Chinese coal production. Nuclear Energy (up 6.72%) and Wind Energy (up 7.47%) also saw solid performance, suggesting broad investor interest across the traditional-renewable spectrum to achieve energy transition goals.

Solar Energy, however, lagged at 5.10%, pointing to investor caution amid policy uncertainty and a slowdown in new utility-scale installations following the rollback of clean energy subsidies.

Stablecoins Go Mainstream: The Corporate Adoption Wave

Fueled by landmark regulation, notably the U.S. GENIUS Act, the stablecoin market—expected to grow beyond USD 190 billion—is shifting from speculative assets to an essential backbone of the crypto economy. Global corporations are evaluating stablecoin payment integrations to streamline costs and enhance international settlements, particularly in emerging economies.

In parallel, Europe’s MiCA regulation is unlocking stablecoin activity across the continent, offering legal clarity for euro-denominated tokens and driving participation from traditional financial institutions. Major banks are piloting stablecoin issuance, custody and trading services to modernize payments and reduce reliance on legacy rails.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: A Better Quarter Than Headline Numbers
data.description:
Today, I am putting ADMA Biologics, Inc. (NASDAQ:ADMA) in the spotlight for the first time in 2025. This unique healthcare concern saw its stock stage a significant pullback after posting sector quarter results after the bell on Wednesday of this week. The stock ended down nearly 10% at market close Thursday, after being down 20% early in the trading day. Buying opportunity or is the stock not in the 'buy zone' yet? An analysis follows below.

ADMA Biologics, Inc. is headquartered in New Jersey. The company operates a network of 10 plasma collection facilities in the U.S., the last of which was completed in 2023. It manufactures two key products from this collection, BIVIGAM and ASCENIV. With the post quarterly earnings plunge in the stock, the shares trade for around $16.50 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization just north of $4 billion.

Second Quarter Results:

ADMA Biologics, Inc. posted its Q2 numbers on August 6th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of 14 cents a share, in line with the consensus. GAAP net income for the quarter came in at $34.2 million, a seven percent increase over 2Q2024. Importantly, adjusted EBITDA rose 59% to $50.8 million not including a non-recurring item (a $12.6 million Medicaid rebate accrual reversal) that occurred in the same period a year ago. Gross margins improved to 55.1% from 53.6% but would have rose 7.7% if not for that non-recurring item from the prior period a year ago. Adjusted net income was $36 million, up 89% from 2Q2024 sans that non-recuring item.

Revenues rose nearly 14% on a year-over-year basis to $122 million, just a tad over expectations. Revenues would have been up 29% over the same quarter a year ago except for that non-recurring item in the previous period. The company did not provide a sales breakdown, but the majority of revenues came from ASCENIV. Importantly, during the quarter, the company initiated an FDA approved manufacturing change that will provide a 20% yield enhancement.

Management reaffirmed FY2025 and FY2026 guidance around all key metrics including revenue, net income and adjusted EBITDA. Leadership also projected it can achieve $1.1 billion in annual revenues by FY2030. In addition, management also clarified that 'Potential upside from the FDA-approved yield enhancement process remains excluded from 2025 guidance and is conservatively reflected in 2026 projections'. The same applied to the FY2030 $1.1 billion revenue projection. Nearer term, management expects at least $625 million in total revenue, adjusted EBITDA of $340 million or more and adjusted net income of at least $245 million in FY2026.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

Approximately six percent of the outstanding float in the shares are currently held short. Insiders and beneficial owners of the stock have sold just over $10 million collectively worth of equity so far in 2025. Analyst firms have not yet chimed in since second quarter results hit the wires. Following first quarter results, Cantor Fitzgerald ($25 price target), Mizuho Securities ($35 price target, up previously from $32) and Raymond James ($32 price target, up from $25 previously) all reissued Buy ratings.

After the quarter closed, ADMA Biologics executed a syndicated debt refinancing led by J.P. Morgan (effective August 5th). Management stated this refinancing 'should meaningfully reduce ADMA's borrowing costs while enhancing liquidity and strategic optionality through a new $225 million revolving credit facility'.

The company listed just over $90 million of cash and a tad over $73 million in senior notes on its balance in the 10-K, management filed for Q2. In addition, the company implemented a $500 million stock buyback authorization in the second quarter, that would take approximately 12% of the shares out of the float at current trading levels. ADMA Biologics bought back $15 million of its own stock in Q2.

Conclusion:

ADMA Biologics made 49 cents a share on just over $426 million in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has earnings rising to 58 cents a share in FY2025 as sales climb to $506 million. They project sales to move up substantially to 93 cents a share in FY2026 on revenue growth of 25%.

The stock seems to have been unfairly punished on Thursday as ADMA Biologics only met top and bottom-line expectations. Margins are improving impressively and should increase additional on new improved manufacturing yield. Guidance for FY2025 and FY2026 is likely to be taken up once the full impacts of the more effective process are fully known.

The company has more favorable financing and just put in place a large stock buyback program. Revenue over the next couple of years will be in the low to mid 20s and ADMA Biologics will become increasing profitable at a solid clip. In summary, yesterday's market action seems a bit of an overreaction. ADMA remains a solid 'accumulate' and I added to my stake in the stock via covered call orders early Thursday when the shares briefly dipped under $15.00 a share.

data.title: fuboTV: This Show Isn't Over Just Yet
data.description:
August 8th was a really good day for shareholders of fuboTV Inc. (NYSE:FUBO). Shares of the company rose around 5% after management announced, before the market opened, financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Management reported revenue, earnings per share, and adjusted earnings per share, that all exceeded analysts’ expectations. On top of this, subscriber numbers came in better than expected, and management is looking forward to completing its merger with Hulu + Live TV in a deal that will see The Walt Disney Company (DIS) come to own 70% of the combined business.

Back in May of this year, I called the company a Buy candidate. Since then, the stock has skyrocketed by 65.7%, easily outperforming the 12.9% rise that the S&P 500 saw. This move higher comes amidst improving financial results, at least in some respects, and the realization that the combination of the company with Disney’s Hulu + Live TV will prove to be a game changer that will create value for shareholders in the long run. Even though I think the easy money has been made by now, I do think that the company deserves a bit more upside. Because of this, I think that having it rated a soft ‘buy’ is logical at this time.

A solid quarter

Fundamentally speaking, fuboTV is doing better than what analysts have been anticipating. For the second quarter of 2025, revenue came in at $380 million. That does represent a 2.8% drop from the $391 million the company reported one year earlier. However, it happens to be $12.9 million above what analysts were expecting.

This drop in sales came as the number of subscribers in North America declined from 1.45 million last year to 1.36 million this year. This took North American revenue down from $382.7 million to $371.3 million. Management has stopped reporting monthly ARPU (average revenue per user). But based on my own estimates, it should have risen by about 3.7% year over year. It is worth pointing out that management was only expecting subscribers of between 1.225 million and 1.255 million. So that does mark a significant win for the company. For its international operations, subscriber count dropped from 399,000 to 349,000. Even so, revenue increased 4.7% year over year. But at just $8.7 million for the second quarter of 2025, it is basically a rounding error. Just like with the operations in North America, the international side of the business did better than expected from a subscriber perspective. Management had been anticipating subscribers amounting to between 325,000 and 335,000.

On the bottom line, the company showed some improvements. The company went from losing $0.08 per share to losing $0.02 per share, taking its net loss from $25.3 million to $8 million. The earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.01 better than what analysts anticipated. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, went from negative $0.04 to positive $0.05. It ultimately ended up being $0.02 above what analysts were hoping to see. This translated to arise from negative $13.4 million to positive $18.1 million when it comes to adjusted net profits.

Most other profitability metrics improved also. Operating cash flow was the one exception, worsening from negative $31.9 million to negative $34.9 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $17.2 million to negative $11.3 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA also improved, going from negative $11 million to positive $20.7 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. For that time, revenue for the company actually improved. This was thanks to a strong showing in the first quarter of the year as the company benefited from a rise in ARPU. Bottom line results have gotten better, but some of that is due to a $219.7 million gain on the settlement of litigation that the company booked in the first quarter of the year. This was in relation to the $220 million payment that the company received from Disney in relation to the merger transaction. Because of this, EBITDA is the most appropriate metric here. And that does indeed show strength for the company.

It is important to keep in mind that the firm continues to make progress on improving its margins. In the image above, you can see global expenses as a percentage of total revenue for the business. They have been consistently on the decline since the third quarter of 2024 when they hit 115%. By the first quarter of this year, they had fallen to 106%. And in the most recent quarter, they amounted to 102%. This improvement seems to be centered around subscriber related costs, which management is finding ways to improve. I would venture to say that a lot of this has to do with price increases and new monetized features as opposed to actual cost cutting. Even so, progress is progress all the same.

Unlike in previous quarters, management is not really providing any detailed guidance now. But that's likely because of the upcoming merger with Disney’s Hulu + Live TV business. Management currently expects that transaction to close either in the final quarter of this year or in the first quarter of next year. At the same time, management continues to make investments in the core business. In fact, the firm will soon be launching Fubo Sports, which they have described as a "skinny content service for sports fans." They also recently launched a pay-per-view feature that allows subscribers and non-subscribers to access live events on a one-off basis. I find that to be innovative and exciting, even though I personally am not a sports fan.

The one challenge when it comes to a company like fuboTV is that we don't really know what to expect of the future. Management has not provided detailed guidance recently regarding what the post-merger business should look like. Taking from my previous article, you can see in the image above what management's prior expectations were for the combined business. Assuming that this still holds, we can expect a firm that is drastically larger than it is at the moment. Even if the $120 million in annual run rate synergies don't come to fruition, we are still looking at a business that is generating significant and growing cash flows as revenue expands.

To understand what the picture of the business might look like on a combined basis, I created the chart above. In it, you can see, both with synergies and without synergies, what the implied EV to EBITDA multiple of the company should be based on current pricing. Even if we assume that synergies don't come to fruition, the stock is not exactly expensive. And their synergies are realized, which could happen but are not guaranteed, shares could look rather cheap, especially as we move forward a couple of years. It is the realization of this value here, I believe, that has market participants so excited recently. After all, the company on a standalone basis is not that appealing. Remember that, from when I first wrote about the firm in February of 2024 until I subsequently upgraded it in January of this year, I had it rated a Sell.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I think that the market has finally come to realize the opportunity that exists with fuboTV Inc. Since I upgraded the stock, shares have risen materially. I would argue that the easy money has already been made now. But I do think that, as the merger nears completion, the firm will deserve some additional upside. Based on the data provided, I do believe that maintaining it as a soft Buy candidate is appropriate right now
